sections.title: Regional Cuisines: A Land of Culinary Diversity
sections.body: Spain's cuisine varies dramatically by region, shaped by geography and history. The Basque Country is renowned for pintxos and refined seafood cooking, while Galicia excels in octopus and shellfish thanks to its Atlantic coastline. Andalusia offers fried fish, cold soups, and Moorish-influenced flavors, and Catalonia blends French and Mediterranean techniques with rustic mountain fare.

sections.title: Signature Dishes You Must Try
sections.body: Paella, originally from Valencia, showcases rice cooked with saffron, vegetables, and meat or seafood. Jamón ibérico, cured for months from acorn-fed pigs, is a national treasure enjoyed thinly sliced. Tortilla española, a simple potato and egg omelette, appears everywhere from bars to home kitchens, and gazpacho offers a refreshing cold tomato soup perfect for hot summers.

sections.title: Tapas Culture and Small Plates
sections.body: Tapas are small, shareable dishes meant to accompany drinks and encourage socializing rather than solitary dining. Bars often serve free tapas with a drink order in cities like Granada, while elsewhere you order dishes like croquetas, patatas bravas, or boquerones individually. This style of eating encourages hopping between bars, sampling different specialties at each stop.

sections.title: Spanish Wines: From Rioja to Cava
sections.body: Rioja, Spain's most famous wine region, produces excellent reds from Tempranillo grapes, aged in oak for distinctive complexity. Ribera del Duero offers bold, structured reds, while Catalonia's Penedès region is celebrated for Cava, Spain's sparkling wine made using the traditional method. Sherry from Jerez, ranging from bone-dry Fino to sweet Pedro Ximénez, remains an underappreciated treasure worth exploring.

sections.title: Dining Customs and Meal Timing
sections.body: Spanish meal times run later than much of Europe, with lunch often starting at 2pm and dinner rarely before 9pm or 10pm. The midday meal is traditionally the largest, sometimes accompanied by a post-lunch siesta in smaller towns. Evening socializing frequently begins with tapas and drinks before a late dinner, reflecting the relaxed, communal pace of Spanish dining culture.

sections.title: Markets and Local Ingredients
sections.body: Vibrant markets like Madrid's Mercado de San Miguel or Barcelona's La Boqueria showcase Spain's obsession with fresh, quality ingredients. Olive oil, produced abundantly in Andalusia, forms the backbone of Spanish cooking, while regional cheeses like Manchego add distinctive character to meals. Seasonal produce and local sourcing remain central to Spanish culinary identity, even in modern restaurant settings.

sections.title: Sweet Endings: Spanish Desserts
sections.body: Churros with thick hot chocolate make a beloved breakfast or late-night treat, especially in Madrid. Turrón, a nougat confection with almonds and honey, is traditionally associated with Christmas but enjoyed year-round. Crema catalana, similar to crème brûlée, and flan showcase Spain's love of custard-based desserts with a caramelized touch.
